name: Biodiversity Route
name_original: Torre de Belém a partir do Mosteiro dos Jerónimos — percurso circular em Lisboa
description: This easy 3.4-mile circular hike connects Lisbon's iconic Mosteiro dos Jerónimos and Torre de Belém along the Tagus River.
region_intro: Hiking around Santa Maria De Belém offers a blend of urban walks, scenic riverside paths, and access to significant green spaces. The district is situated along the Tagus River, providing flat promenades and views. While primarily urban, it borders Monsanto Forest Park, Lisbon's largest urban green expanse, featuring woodlands and lookouts. The terrain is generally low-lying, with minimal elevation gain, making most routes accessible. Best hiking trails around Santa Maria De Belém The most popular hiking route is Biodiversity Route, a 3.4 miles (5.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 25 minutes to complete. This easy route explores natural elements within the urban environment. Another top favourite among local hikers is Belém Tower – Jerónimos Monastery loop from Belém, a moderate 5.4 miles (8.7 km) path. This route connects two of Belém's most iconic historical landmarks. Local hikers also love the Belém Tower – View of Ponte 25 de Abril loop from Belém, an 8.2 miles (13.2 km) trail leading through riverside promenades and past significant viewpoints, often completed in about 3 hours 25 minutes. Hiking around Santa Maria De Belém is defined by riverside paths, urban green spaces, and historical landmarks. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy strolls to moderate urban explorations. The routes in Santa Maria De Belém are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 500 reviews. More than 6000 hikers have used komoot to explore Santa Maria De Belém's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: There are over 25 hiking routes in Santa Maria De Belém, offering a variety of experiences from easy urban strolls to moderate explorations. The komoot community has explored these trails over 6,000 times.

Q: Are there easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or casual walkers in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Yes, Santa Maria De Belém is very accommodating for beginners and casual walkers. Out of the available routes, 21 are classified as easy, featuring minimal elevation gain and well-maintained paths. These often include riverside promenades and walks through local gardens.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Yes, many routes in Santa Maria De Belém are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10346197" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belém Tower – Jerónimos Monastery loop from Belém&lt;/a&gt; is a popular moderate circular path connecting two iconic landmarks.

Q: Are there family-friendly hiking options in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Absolutely. The district's flat riverside promenades and numerous gardens, such as the Jardim do Ultramar and the Tropical Botanical Garden, are ideal for families. Many of the easy routes are suitable for all ages, offering pleasant walks without significant challenges.

Q: Are dogs allowed on hiking trails in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Yes, many areas in Santa Maria De Belém are dog-friendly, especially the riverside paths and open green spaces like Monsanto Forest Park, which borders the district. Always keep your dog on a leash and be mindful of local regulations and other visitors.

Q: What kind of landscapes and natural features can I expect to see while hiking in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Hiking in Belém offers a mix of scenic riverside views along the Tagus River, lush urban gardens, and pathways through the edges of Monsanto Forest Park. You'll encounter diverse flora, open green spaces, and the expansive river, providing a refreshing natural backdrop to the historic surroundings.

Q: What historical landmarks can I explore on a hike in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Belém is rich in history. You can incorporate iconic landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4087508" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Belém Tower&lt;/a&gt;, the Jerónimos Monastery, and the Monument to the Discoveries into your walks. The Hermitage of Restelo also offers stunning views over the Tagus River and Belém Tower.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: The routes in Santa Maria De Belém are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 500 reviews. Hikers often praise the beautiful riverside views, the historical significance of the landmarks, and the accessibility of the paths for various fitness levels.

Q: Are there any routes that offer great viewpoints in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Yes, several routes provide excellent viewpoints. The riverside promenades offer continuous views of the Tagus River and the 25 de Abril Bridge. The Hermitage of Restelo, situated on a hilltop, also provides sweeping vistas over the river and the Belém Tower.

Q: How can I get to the hiking trails in Santa Maria De Belém using public transport?
A: Santa Maria De Belém is well-served by public transport. You can reach the district easily by tram (Tram 15E), bus, or train from central Lisbon. These options drop you within walking distance of many trailheads and key attractions like the Jerónimos Monastery and Belém Tower.

Q: Is there parking available for hikers in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: Yes, there are several parking options in Santa Maria De Belém, including paid street parking and underground car parks, particularly near major attractions like the Jerónimos Monastery and the Belém Tower. It's advisable to arrive early, especially during peak tourist seasons, to secure a spot.

Q: Are there any hidden gems or less-known trails in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: While many trails focus on the main landmarks, exploring the smaller gardens like the Tropical Botanical Garden offers a peaceful retreat. Additionally, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1380950989" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Biodiversity Route&lt;/a&gt; is an easy trail that explores natural elements within the urban environment, providing a unique perspective away from the busiest spots.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Santa Maria De Belém?
A: The best time to hike in Santa Maria De Belém is during spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) when the weather is mild and pleasant. Summers can be hot, making early mornings or late afternoons preferable, while winters are generally mild but can be rainy.
tips: The Navy Museum in Lisbon, also known as Museu de Marinha, is an emblematic place that immerses us in the rich Portuguese maritime history, especially during the Age of Discoveries.
Built for the great exhibition of the Portuguese world in 1940, it is characterized by its large lawn areas.

Occupying approximately 2 hectares, this municipal garden has garden areas surrounded by hedges, rest areas with large open spaces, four small artificial lakes and sculptures. Among them, there is a monument in homage to the Vice-King of India, Afonso de Albuquerque, designed by the sculptor Costa Mota.

The Monument to the Discoveries, popularly known as Padrão dos Descobrimentos, is a monument built in 1960, on the banks of the Tagus River, in Belém, Lisbon, to commemorate the 500th anniversary of the death of Henry the Navigator.
This is a comparatively small pseudo lighthouse between the Belm Tower and the Monument to the Discoveries.
Monument is dedicated to Sacadura Cabral and Gago Coutinholos, who were the first Portuguese aviators to cross the South Atlantic in 1922

The Torre de Belem is the most characteristic building in Lisbon, being the monument in Portugal that comes closest to instant recognition. This delightfully tiny fortress has guarded the Tagus Estuary since the 16th century and was built in the heyday of the Manueline style of architecture.

The Fort of Bom Sucesso is located slightly to the west of the Tower of Belém in Belém in the municipality of Lisbon, in Portugal. It currently houses the Museu do Combatente and the Monument to the Overseas Combatants.

faqs.question: How long does it take to complete this circular route?
faqs.answer: The route is approximately 5.54 kilometers (3.44 miles) long and typically takes around 1 hour and 25 minutes to complete at a comfortable walking pace. This duration is an estimate and can vary depending on how much time you spend exploring the landmarks.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route is rated as 'easy' and is very suitable for beginners and families. It features flat, paved paths and sidewalks with minimal elevation gain, making it a comfortable stroll for most people.

faqs.question: Where does the route start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route is circular and can be started from various points, but a common starting point is near the Jerónimos Monastery. Parking can be found in the Belém district, though it can be busy. Consider public transport for ease.

faqs.question: How can I get to the trailhead using public transport?
faqs.answer: The Belém district is well-served by public transport in Lisbon. You can reach the area near the Jerónimos Monastery by tram (e.g., Tram 15E), bus, or train (Belém station). This is often the most convenient way to access the start of the route.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this walk?
faqs.answer: The terrain is predominantly flat, consisting of paved paths and sidewalks. You'll be walking along the scenic banks of the Tagus River, through beautifully maintained gardens and open plazas, making for a very pleasant urban walk.

faqs.question: What are the main landmarks and points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in history and culture. You'll pass by iconic sites such as the Jerónimos Monastery, the Monument to the Discoveries, and the Belém Tower. Other highlights include the Vasco da Gama Garden and the Bom Sucesso Fort (Combatant's Museum).

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to walk this route?
faqs.answer: Spring (April-May) and early autumn (September-October) are generally the best times to walk this route. The weather is pleasant, not too hot, and the crowds are typically smaller than in peak summer. Lisbon's mild winters can also be suitable, though you might encounter more rain.

faqs.question: Is this circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route itself is on public sidewalks and parks, specific rules for dogs may apply in certain areas or within the grounds of monuments. Generally, dogs on a leash are permitted in public outdoor spaces, but always check local signage, especially in gardens or near historical sites.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this walk?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to walk the circular route itself. However, if you wish to enter the Jerónimos Monastery or the Belém Tower, there will be separate entrance fees.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route overlaps with several other paths. You'll find yourself on sections of the Eixos principais CML - Eixo Marginal, the EuroVelo 1 - Atlantic Coast Route - part Portugal 9, and the Trilho das Areias, among others.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or get refreshments along the route?
faqs.answer: Absolutely! The Belém district is famous for its culinary offerings. You'll find numerous cafes and restaurants, including places to try the renowned Pastéis de Belém custard tarts, especially near the Jerónimos Monastery and along the riverside.

waypoints.name: Monument to Gago Coutinho and Sacadura Cabral
